lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: My former speeches have but hit your thoughts,

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Which can interpret further: only, I say,

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Things have been strangely borne. The gracious Duncan

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Was pitied of Macbeth: marry, he was dead:

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: And the right-valiant Banquo walk’d too late;

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Whom, you may say, if’t please you, Fleance kill’d,

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: For Fleance fled: men must not walk too late.

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Who cannot want the thought how monstrous

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: It was for Malcolm and for Donalbain

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: To kill their gracious father? damned fact!

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: How it did grieve Macbeth! did he not straight

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: In pious rage the two delinquents tear,

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: That were the slaves of drink and thralls of sleep?

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Was not that nobly done? Ay, and wisely too;

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: For ’twould have anger’d any heart alive

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: To hear the men deny’t. So that, I say,

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: He has borne all things well: and I do think

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: That had he Duncan’s sons under his key—

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: As, an’t please heaven, he shall not—they should find

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: What ’twere to kill a father; so should Fleance.

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: But, peace! for from broad words and ’cause he fail’d

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: His presence at the tyrant’s feast, I hear

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Macduff lives in disgrace: sir, can you tell

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Where he bestows himself?

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: The son of Duncan,

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: From whom this tyrant holds the due of birth,

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Lives in the English court, and is received

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Of the most pious Edward with such grace

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: That the malevolence of fortune nothing

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Takes from his high respect: thither Macduff

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Is gone to pray the holy king, upon his aid

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: To wake Northumberland and warlike Siward:

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: That, by the help of these—with Him above

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: To ratify the work—we may again

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Give to our tables meat, sleep to our nights,

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Free from our feasts and banquets bloody knives,

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Do faithful homage and receive free honours:

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: All which we pine for now: and this report

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Hath so exasperate the king that he

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: Prepares for some attempt of war.

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Sent he to Macduff?

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: He did: and with an absolute “Sir, not I,”

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: The cloudy messenger turns me his back,

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: And hums, as who should say “You’ll rue the time

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: That clogs me with this answer.”

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: And that well might

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Advise him to a caution, to hold what distance

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: His wisdom can provide. Some holy angel

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Fly to the court of England and unfold

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: His message ere he come, that a swift blessing

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: May soon return to this our suffering country

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Under a hand accursed!

lines.speaker: LORD
lines.text: I’ll send my prayers with him.

glosses.definition: the play's first direct application of the word to Macbeth — a political charge
surly messenger returning with bad news

annotations.title: The political assessment
annotations.body:
Lennox's long speech to a 'Lord' is the play's first moment of explicit political analysis. The word 'tyrant' is applied to Macbeth for the first time here. Lennox runs through Macbeth's rapid body count — Duncan, the grooms, now Banquo — and lets the repetitions speak: 'the gracious Duncan,' 'the right valiant Banquo,' 'the two delinquents.'

The irony is sustained. Lennox speaks as if praising Macbeth's story, but every sentence is a small accusation. The technique is one of the play's most compressed pieces of political speech: what cannot be said aloud in court is said in the form of ostentatious agreement. The scene is a preview of the discretion Scottish nobles will require until Malcolm's army arrives.

annotations.title: Edward the Confessor and the political theology of kingship
annotations.body:
The Lord reports that Macduff has fled to England, to the court of Edward the Confessor (r. 1042–1066), the English king venerated for his piety. Edward's capacity to cure the 'King's Evil' (scrofula) by royal touch — staged in 4.3 — is the counter-image to Macbeth's tyranny: the legitimate king has divine power to heal; the usurper has only the power to kill.

The reference is political. James I practiced the royal touch ceremony, reviving a tradition the Puritans had tried to suppress. The play's 1606 audience, seeing Edward's healing at work, would have seen an argument for the divine right of kings that James had explicitly made in his *True Law of Free Monarchies* (1598). Macbeth's tyranny is the argument's negative space.

trials.prompt: Lennox's ironic speech about Duncan, Banquo, and the 'delinquents' functions as:
options: Political speech-in-code — saying what cannot be said aloud by way of ostentatious agreement

trials.prompt: Edward the Confessor's touch-healing in the play frames:
options: An argument for divine right of kings — the legitimate king heals, the usurper only kills
